title: Nephrolithiasis: Diagnosis and Management
objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of renal colic
Understand stone composition and risk factors
Apply appropriate imaging and laboratory workup

sections.title: Epidemiology & Risk Factors
sections.content:
**Prevalence:** 10-15% lifetime risk

**Risk Factors:**
• Low fluid intake (most important modifiable factor)
• High sodium diet
• High animal protein intake
• Obesity and metabolic syndrome
• Family history (2.5x risk)
• Hot climates (dehydration)
• Certain medications (topiramate, indinavir)

**Recurrence:** 50% within 5-10 years without intervention

sections.title: Stone Composition
sections.content:
**Calcium Oxalate (75-80%):**
• Most common type
• Envelope-shaped crystals on UA
• Associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria

**Calcium Phosphite (15%):**
• Associated with RTA type 1
• Alkaline urine

**Uric Acid (5-10%):**
• Radiolucent on X-ray
• Acidic urine (pH <5.5)
• Gout, myeloproliferative disorders
• Can be dissolved with alkalinization

**Struvite (5-10%):**
• 'Coffin lid' crystals
• UTI with urease-producing bacteria (Proteus)
• Can form staghorn calculi

**Cystine (1-2%):**
• Hexagonal crystals
• Autosomal recessive cystinuria
• Positive cyanide-nitroprusside test

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Renal Colic:**
• Sudden onset, severe, colicky flank pain
• Radiates to groin/testicle/labia
• Patient unable to find comfortable position
• Nausea and vomiting
• Hematuria (80-90%)

**Pain Location by Stone Position:**
• Upper ureter/UPJ: Flank pain
• Mid-ureter: Lower quadrant pain
• Distal ureter/UVJ: Suprapubic, dysuria, urgency

**Red Flags:**
• Fever (infected stone - emergency!)
• Solitary kidney
• Bilateral obstruction
• Acute kidney injury

sections.title: Diagnostic Workup
sections.content:
**Imaging:**
• Non-contrast CT (gold standard) - 95% sensitivity
• KUB X-ray: Limited, misses radiolucent stones
• Ultrasound: First-line in pregnancy, children

**Laboratory:**
• Urinalysis: Hematuria, pH, crystals
• BMP: Creatinine, electrolytes
• CBC: Infection screening
• Urine culture if febrile

**24-Hour Urine Collection:**
• For recurrent stone formers
• Identifies metabolic abnormalities
• Guides preventive therapy

sections.title: Management - Conservative
sections.content:
**Medical Expulsive Therapy (MET):**
• Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin 0.4mg daily)
• Increases spontaneous passage rate
• Most effective for distal ureteral stones

**Spontaneous Passage Rates by Size:**
• <5mm: 68-98% pass spontaneously
• 5-10mm: 47% pass
• >10mm: Unlikely to pass

**Supportive Care:**
• Aggressive hydration
• NSAIDs (ketorolac) - first-line analgesia
• Antiemetics
• Strain urine for stone analysis

sections.title: Management - Surgical
sections.content:
**Indications for Intervention:**
• Stone >10mm
• Failure to progress after 4-6 weeks
• Uncontrolled pain
• Infection with obstruction (EMERGENCY)
• Acute kidney injury
• Solitary kidney

**Options:**
• **SWL (Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy):** Non-invasive, stones <2cm
• **Ureteroscopy:** Direct visualization, laser lithotripsy
• **PCNL:** Large/staghorn calculi

**Emergency Decompression:**
• Infected obstructing stone = sepsis risk
• Ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube
• Antibiotics before definitive treatment

sections.title: Prevention
sections.content:
**Universal Recommendations:**
• Increase fluid intake (>2.5L urine output/day)
• Reduce sodium intake
• Moderate animal protein
• Maintain normal calcium intake (not low!)

**Stone-Specific Prevention:**
• Calcium oxalate: Thiazide diuretics, potassium citrate
• Uric acid: Alkalinize urine (K-citrate), allopurinol
• Cystine: High fluid, alkalinization, tiopronin
• Struvite: Treat/prevent UTIs, complete stone removal

keyTakeaways: Non-contrast CT is the gold standard for diagnosis
Infected obstructing stone is a urologic emergency
24-hour urine collection guides prevention in recurrent formers
Increased fluid intake is the most important preventive measure

references.keyContent: Comprehensive overview of kidney stone diagnosis and management

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Surgical Management of Stones
AUA Guideline: Medical Management of Kidney Stones

reviewQuestions.question: A 35-year-old man presents with acute left flank pain, nausea, and hematuria. CT shows a 4mm distal ureteral stone. He is afebrile with normal renal function. What is the most appropriate initial management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: A 4mm distal ureteral stone has a high likelihood of spontaneous passage (>90%). Medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin, adequate hydration, and pain control is appropriate first-line management. Surgery is reserved for failure to progress or complications.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with a known ureteral stone develops fever to 39°C and rigors. Labs show leukocytosis and elevated creatinine. What is the most important next step?
options: Start IV antibiotics and schedule outpatient ureteroscopy
Emergency ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube placement
reviewQuestions.explanation: Infected obstructing stone is a urologic emergency with high risk of sepsis and death. Emergency decompression with ureteral stent or nephrostomy tube is required, along with IV antibiotics. Definitive stone treatment occurs after infection resolves.

reviewQuestions.question: Which stone type is radiolucent on plain radiograph and can potentially be dissolved with medical therapy?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Uric acid stones are radiolucent on X-ray (visible only on CT) and form in acidic urine. They can be dissolved by alkalinizing the urine to pH 6.5-7.0 with potassium citrate. This is unique among common stone types.
